speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five. January 6, 1997. [INAUDIBLE] interviewing Stan Rubens. So your parents came to visit you a couple of times, but did you send them any mail, or did they send you mail?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no communication. I was-- literally, my sister was there, and she and I, we were on our own. And it is-- it was interesting how my sister was kind of on her own and stayed very privately and how I stayed very privately. We-- we just literally played it cool, and we didn't want to draw attention to ourselves. We-- we stayed very quiet. We didn't want anybody to know we were there, basically speaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- as a child, did you have dreams at night about your future, about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- as a matter of fact, a few years in the war, I started getting dreams. Then the dreams actually became nightmares. That followed me, until I came to a-- and still they happen from time to time. But I resolved my nightmares, actually, for 90% in-- when I moved to Australia. But I did have regular nightmares of being in an area and feeling myself like sinking through the ground, or I'd be in the water and sinking down, or in a swamp or anything, it was-- or in a tree, falling out of a tree. There was always this moment where I had a feeling that I was being sucked down or sucked under or disappearing through the ground. Could be a forest, could be anything. And then I would wake up, and I would say, OK, and I would fall asleep again. But I had nightmares on a continuous basis, yeah. Though those nightmares still plague me, but I can deal with them now much better, because I know what caused them, and I found that out in Australia. I was-- but I might be getting ahead of-- losing track of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you can tell that. We'll go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When I-- when I moved to Australia, I came to live with-- with a Dr. van der Portens, where I had rented a room. I was sharing a room. He had left Holland-- and also a Jewish person-- and had gone to Australia so that he could practice in Australia. He couldn't practice yet. He had to still pass his exam. During the time, he had a house in which he rented out rooms. I got a room there. And one day he said to me, he said, you know-- that-- by then my name became Stan-- he said, I-- I have some books I think you should read. And he gave me books of Freud, Jung, Adler, very deep psychology. And I started-- I was-- I was 19, turning 20, and I started reading those books. And in one of those books, I was reading about how you could resolve your dreams through free association. And I had, again, one of those dreams, and I went through the free association process by myself and realized that every time I was reading-- I was having those nightmares, it dealt with the fact that I was chased by the German, when he chased me, and how I [INAUDIBLE]-- paralyzing-- with my paralyzing fear, made me drop to the floor. And that's where this whole thing came from. After I found the explanation of this-- of this dream, I remember helping other-- many other people solving their dreams who had nightmares. And it was a very interesting experience, a very interesting time in my   life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio on the farm where you were staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't have any radios, but we had a black-- black market radio that we would tune in to from time to time, because the Germans were very anxious to find any radio they could find. So we would only listen to it for a very short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you listen to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We listened to the BBC. And I remember the big bang-- the big-- big band sounds of the Beethoven's-- what is it-- 'Unfinished'? And then the sound of [INAUDIBLE] is Radio Vrij Nederland, Radio Free Netherlands, Radio Oranje, and listened to the reports. And yeah, we had maps on the wall, trying to keep track of where the Allies were and, of course, besides ourselves when finally we were liberated by the-- mostly by the Canadian-- Canadians.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- do you remember Liberation Day?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a degree I remember it, because I went into Utrecht. I was-- everybody went into Utrecht to-- because we knew that the Canadians were coming in. And that was a marvelous, unbelievable experience [LAUGHS] to see those cars coming  in, yeah. And-- so they-- I don't know, it was just a beautiful experience to be free again and walk around and do what you wanted to do. Anyhow, we went into-- I remember going into Utrecht a couple of times. And then my father said, well, I'm going back to Amsterdam. And he and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father connect with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well they came over to the-- they came over to the farmhouse and said, well-- my father, he was a funny guy in a way. It's easy to-- to talk him down because of the many unpleasant things that he did and the unpleasant experience we had. On the other hand, I have to keep in mind that he was under tremendous pressure. He felt very guilty that his mother, sister, and his cousin were hauled away by the Germans. That-- that-- that really tore at him. And it may have been one of the things why he was so incredibly moody in his way. And he had really suffered from that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, one day my father said, OK, we are going to Amsterdam. And I said, yeah, I said. And you can follow in a couple of days. I said, how do I get there? And he looked at me, he said, walk. I said, OK. He said, here's 10 guldens and just find me in the store and go to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, OK, never thinking twice of how I was going to get from Achttienhoven after war-torn Holland, how I would go, how I would walk. I figured, hey, I'm liberated. I'm free. I can do what I want to do. I was as happy as a clam. I said, I'll get there. And he said, very laconic, without further thinking, he said, OK, I'll see you there. And I don't even remember what my sister did. All I remember is that one day I said to Tante Beppie, I said well, I think I better go to Amsterdam. And she said, well, OK, I'll make you a sandwich. And I got up early in the morning, and I just took off in the direction of Amsterdam. I knew I had to go that way and that way and that way, and there was sign posts all over the place. So that was the least of my problems. And I had started to walk. I guess it must have been about 3 o'clock in the afternoon, and I was getting a little bit tired. And I said, well, it's getting closer to nighttime. I should-- maybe I can find a farmhouse, somewhere where they would all let me sleep in the haystack. And-- and so I can go. It was May, so the weather was fine. And I figured maybe I can find a place where I can sleep in a haystack and go on tomorrow, and I'll get there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I spoke a little bit English already, because we had been practicing English when I was in Utrecht with my cousin. So I see a Canadian standing at the side of the road in front-- standing guard in some beautiful, beautiful villa, almost like a castle. And I said, hi. And he says, hello, how you doing? Where are you off to? I said, well, I'm trying to get to Amsterdam. But right now I need a place to sleep. Do you think they would let me in there? And he said, oh, you need to get to Amsterdam, eh? I said, yeah, my father. He says, why? I said, well, my father went ahead of me, and my mother is there. So I want to get-- want to get to them. He said, oh. He said, well, OK, just one moment. Stops a car, the car goes through. Stops another car. I mean, he's a military guard, so cars would stop for  him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, OK, come over here. And a little-- I think it was a little Fiat. He said, they are going to Amsterdam, and they'll drop you off wherever you want to. I said, oh, thank you. And an hour later, I was in Amsterdam. I walked into my father's place, where he meanwhile gotten the locksmiths to open his old store, because the [INAUDIBLE] had disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked in his place,  and my father says, oh, you're there? OK. Do that and that and that for  me. Do that and that and that. And he started ordering me around to start cleaning up the store. Never asked me, how did you get here? Are you OK? No,  he just put me to work, said, do that, do that, do that, do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then meanwhile, they had found a place to live in the Dintelstraat [INAUDIBLE], and-- which was a place of the NSB people that had left when the crazy Thursday came about, and everybody got scared that they better get out of the country. Whatever happened to those people, I don't know. I don't even know their name. But we moved into an apartment, and from there, life started in reasonable, normal fashion. Or I started going back to school. And we picked up the pieces of where we had left off.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your apartment on the Apollolaan?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Apollolaan, we couldn't get it back. Other people had moved in. Later off on the Dintelstraat, we moved to the Churchilllaan, right near the-- where the sun-- sundial-- sundial used to be. Isn't there anymore, the sundial. But very close to the Apollo [? hall, ?] Almost at the end of the Churchilllaan, Churchilllaan 213. And we had a downstairs apartment there with a yard in the back. And I had a little room there, front of the house. And we lived there, and I went to school. I went to H-- H-- HBO, [DUTCH], higher so  and so [? otherwise, ?] a teaching-- or school. And things started to go back to a reasonable, normal life. There were still troubles. I had troubles at school in learning, great difficulties in learning. My-- my parents were continuously fighting. They had big arguments. And I think one of the things was that my father was still very troubled and may have taken this out on my mother. So there were big fights and big arguments always. Meanwhile, my sister got sick and tired of the whole thing, and she moved out of the house, which pleased my father to no end because he was very unhappy, and it was one mouth less to feed. He started his business. His business started to-- to work all right. Every day I had to go from school, which finished at 4 o'clock, and go to the store, and I had to make whatever deliveries there were.  I had to make deliveries. I got a bike. My father got me a bike, and I would make deliveries on a bicycle all over Amsterdam. There was just no stopping. Wherever there was a delivery to made-- to be made, I made the delivery on my bicycle. And we had chinaware and crystal and leather goods and all that stuff, more. And people needed presents to be delivered. And I was the token delivery boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people recognize you from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It seems strange. It's like you started life all over again. Nobody recognized me. Nobody said, hey, I remember you, or what happened to you? Wasn't there. It's-- it was-- here were four years or five years out of my life that were literally taken away that didn't seem to exist for a lot of people. I existed, but I did not exist for others. And there was no way of recapturing that. My-- I had no youth to speak of. All I did was just be in a little room most of the time. As a matter of fact, my best time was when I was in Achttienhoven when I could leave and walk into the-- into the meadows, which I would do over and over and over again, except when it got cold. And unfortunately, I didn't have-- hardly had any shoes. I was literally walking on two left wooden shoes-- no, two right wooden shoes, because the left wooden-- the left shoe wouldn't fit me. And I still have a raised arch, that my-- my left shoe is of a different size than my right shoe, because they didn't have shoes for me. But you made do because that's the best there was. So I-- you kept on truckin'. Well, anyhow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends whose parents became Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. No, I did not have that. I didn't have-- but-- we disappeared pretty soon, so I never found out if I-- if there were Nazi people around me. And keep in mind, at that time, I was-- when  I went underground I was eight years old-- nine-- eight or nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the distinction of what was a Nazi or NSB or anything like that, I-- I did not understand-- had a full grasp of that distinction. Except I knew that I should make sure to avoid certain people, and I was told avoid that, avoid that, avoid that, avoid that. That was my-- my prime objective, just to avoid certain people. Anyhow, where were we? After the war you went back to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the music that was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was-- I was 14 years old, and I managed to convince my father to let me take music lessons. And the first instrument that I took was Hawaiian steel guitar, of all instruments. And about, oh, two years later or a year later-- I was 15 years old-- I had my first Hawaiian ensemble, and I played for the school, all the school thing. And then about a year later after that, I re-tuned my Hawaiian steel guitar to a regular guitar because I wasn't happy the way  the steel guitar was developing for me. It didn't satisfy me enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this at what you call the HBO school, like the higher professional education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Higher-- higher [DUTCH] or higher professional--

speaker: interviewer
text: Education?

speaker: interviewee
text: --teaching? Yeah. Yeah, that must have been at that school, yeah. Must have been at that because I was 14 when I went to-- 14 or 15 when I went there, yeah. And yeah, in no time I had my own group, and I was running it like, oh,  we do this, we do this, we do this. And hey, it was a-- it was an eight-piece group I had put together. Three girls-- no, two girls were singing and then five-- and four people playing music, It was six piece. And we were rehearsing, and the music was bloody awful. But we had a lot of fun. And we started playing music left, right, and center, wherever I could, and I started playing my guitar after that. And by then, I'm about 17, and I started putting a band together with piano, drums, and myself guitar. And I put dance evenings together for Magen David, which was a Jewish club of youngsters. And I convinced Rabbi Prins to let me have the upstairs room at the Lekstraat synagogue, where every Sunday at 7 o'clock all the kids would meet, and I was running the band. Eventually, this became like the staple thing to do on a Sunday night for all the Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the band?

speaker: interviewee
text: The band had no name. No,  the band had no name. The-- the Hawaiian band had a name. It was called the Golden Strings. And later on when I worked professionally on Surfers Paradise, the-- the group I-- the three-piece group was called the-- the Flamingo Trio, because we were playing at the flamingo restaurant. But I was cranking out music, mediocre as it must have been. But everybody was happy, and everybody was dancing. And we had a lot of fun till Rabbi Prins felt I was getting too powerful and too popular and literally kicked me out of the synagogue because he didn't like the way things were developing. This was getting so popular and getting a little bit rowdy also. So he felt it was getting out of hand, so he stopped the dances. By then, I was about to leave for Australia. I was-- by now, I was 19 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to another school after you finished at--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I didn't finish that school. I got kicked out of school. Yeah, my father was very happy when Mr. [? Holtzup ?], the-- the principal of the school-- I went to first year of HBO, a second year of HBO, and then I had to repeat it. And in the third year, my-- my marks or my figures were so bad that he said, I don't want you in this school anymore. You better get out. I really don't need people like you here. Little did he know that I had great difficulties in studying at that point in time. I had great difficulties at home, where there were continuous fights and-- and-- and arguments, and I couldn't concentrate. I just couldn't concentrate. And the fact that I could concentrate when it was necessary is because when I came back 10 years later, I had to get a [DUTCH] diploma in order to run my mother's store, and I took some private-- private lessons. And in eight, nine months I went through the whole course that a lot of people sometimes take two or three years and went through it, like you would say, cum laude, with-- with As and Bs. So yeah, given the right atmosphere, I had no difficulties in studying. Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, I got kicked out of school. And my father said, well, that's great. You-- you can work for me. Well, my mother was not too happy about that, and she decided to get me a job somewhere else because she felt I wouldn't learn anything from my dad. Because whenever I asked my dad something about how do you do this, how do you do that, my father said, you're too young to know. He wouldn't-- he wouldn't give me information or whatever. Then she got me a job as an apprentice at a place called Netex, N-E-T-E-X, [? Modelum, ?] which was a manufacturer who made women's jackets and coats. And I literally went through the entire-- I learned everything about the company, even some of the bookkeeping, you might say, and how the company was run. And I didn't get much pay. I think I got $5 a month. But I learned a lot, and it later on helped me a lot in my life when I went to Australia because I claimed I was a cutter. I got myself a job as a cutter, even though I had only seen it, how the cutting was done. And later on, I-- I went to school to learn pattern-making and designing, and that helped me when I came to-- to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't want to stay in Holland for a number of reasons. My parents were having continuous arguments, and I was sick and tired of their fighting. So I said, look, I'm gonna leave. And then the two of you can have your divorce, and you don't have to worry about me. Because they were kind of arguing which-- where I should live, with my mother or my father. So I cut that argument short by saying, I'm going to Australia, and you guys can get your divorce.

speaker: interviewer
text: But why Australia? Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I couldn't get into the United States, where I wanted to go, because that was a three-year waiting list. And I was in a hurry to get out, because I didn't want to go into the army either. Because then I would have to go into the army for three years, and I was afraid I wouldn't learn anything in the army. And I said, I'd-- I'd rather be somewhere else where I can work and make a living for myself than these three years in the Army and be stuck in the Dutch Army here. Of course, the Dutch Army in those years was certainly not very popular, and I'm not sure if it is popular at the moment either. Though the-- it has-- they have reduced the time that you have to spend in the army. But to spend three years in the army, that's a hell of a long time when you had just lost four and a half years in the war. And I felt I didn't want to lose any more time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I managed to get my papers within about-- I was just turning 18, and it took me about eight months, and I got my papers for Australia. And so everybody came to say goodbye to me. I was going to take the train from Centraal Station to go to Marseilles where I would pick up a ship called the and where I would go and board that would sail to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the last words of my father when I was there at the station, he says, OK, show me you're worth something. And that was his goodbye to me. So I went to Australia, 19 years old, no profession really. I-- I arrived with $50 in my pocket. I had a room. Nobody came to pick me up from the-- from-- when I arrived because there was nobody there. Except I had a telegram from a cousin of mine who was too far away to pick me up and said, I got a room for you there [INAUDIBLE], which was at the [INAUDIBLE]. And I-- I had some names of people that maybe that might help me. I had no job. I got there, and within one week, I managed to get a job in a-- a distributor-- winery, beer wholesaler in downtown Sydney. And I won't give you the-- the trip of the which was a migrant ship and was a terrible place. It was terrible, smelly. And I remember one of the engineers saying, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] like the devil was on the ship because everything broke down. As a matter of fact, we were for four days in the Indian Ocean stuck with a broken-down engine. Anyhow, they repaired it, and we got to Sydney after about 30, 33 days at sea. Anyhow, I started working there, and I lost the job. Actually, the first year that I was in Sydney I had 13 jobs. I remember distinctly because I had to fill in my tax papers, and there was not enough room to put all the jobs that I had been in. Couldn't find anything that worked for me. And finally, I got-- I got a job as a waiter. And I-- I had never been a waiter, and I didn't know a darn thing about being a waiter. But my cousin, meanwhile, had told me that-- what to do as a waiter. So I got a job in the Blue Mountains in-- in Leura about 60 miles outside of Sydney in a [? pension ?] run by Hungarian people. And I said, well, I said, I-- I can come as a waiter, I said. But I had one suitcase that had all my waiter clothing in it, and I don't have any clothes left at the moment because that-- that case was stolen from me. So I need to get some new clothes before I can go there. And he said, well, OK, get yourself some clothes, and we'll accept you. So I got this job. Actually, I got this job through the Jewish Advisory Board, had a Jewish Advisory Board there, and they helped me to get that job.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Leura, and I'll never forget that first day. And I was given my station, and I said, well, I got to pretend like I'm a waiter. And I walked up to the-- to the kitchen door, and I said, three soups, like I had done it all my life. And carried three soups to whatever place I had to carry them and just carried on from there. I worked about from-- worked from the morning till-- till night, because they were very nice about giving tips. So I started to make a little bit of money, and I started to get ahead in my life. You talked about Surfers Paradise before. Where's the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. As I was-- as I was a waiter, I didn't enjoy really that much being a waiter because I was getting a lot of-- pardon my French-- shit from the cooks. So one day, I-- I looked in the newspaper, and they were always looking for cooks. Waiters-- eh, a dime a dozen. But cooks, they were always short of cooks. And I said to myself, if I want to protect myself-- you got to keep in mind that I'd been through the war with no food. And if you're close to the fire, you won't go hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the logic in my mind, hey, as long as I'm in the kitchen, I can eat. So I said to myself, you know, I should become a cook. I went to a friend of mine in King's Cross, and-- or an acquaintance, and I said, really-- he was from Austria-- and I said, is it OK if I spend some time with you in the kitchen and watch you? Because I'm-- I'm very fortunate. If I see a person do something once, I can repeat it. So I said, can I watch you, and maybe you can tell me a few things of what you're doing? I want to become a cook. Oh, yes, Stan, of course, no problem, come in anytime you want to. So I spent about three evenings or four evenings. Whenever I had some spare time, I would go into the kitchen and watch him what he was doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, OK, about-- after about one week I applied for a job of a cook. And I went to David Jones, which is Liberty House equivalent, or a Bay Company equivalent, or a Bay [INAUDIBLE] equivalent, whatever you want to call it. I went  to the cook, and I said, I would like a job as an assistant cook. And he had an ad in the paper for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, well, what is your experience? I said, well, let me say it like that. I used to work in Holland as an assistant cook. But I'm sure that you have your own way, the way you want things done. Now, you only have to tell me once, and I'll do it your way. He starts to laugh, and he said, well, OK. So he says, are you Jewish? I said to myself, oh, my god, how am I going to answer that question? I'm  in Australia, and this guy is asking me if I am Jewish. And I look at him, and he has a very friendly smile on his face. He said,  are you Jewish? And I said, yes. I said, oh, I said, yeah, I used to work in Israel years ago. I said, I did a lot of kosher cooking. Said, OK, he said, you're hired.
